players.name: Dayne St. Clair
players.bio: Pickering-born shot-stopper who made the move to Inter Miami CF, bringing big-game composure to Les Rouges. With 20 caps, St. Clair is ready to shine on home soil at his first World Cup.
players.facts: St. Clair was born in Pickering, Ontario — a town better known for its nuclear plant than its footballers.

players.name: Alistair Johnston
players.bio: Vancouver's own overlapping right-back who conquered Scottish football with Celtic, winning multiple league titles. A relentless engine on the flank, Johnston is one of Canada's most capped active defenders at just 27.
players.facts: Johnston won back-to-back Scottish Premiership titles with Celtic — not bad for a lad from Vancouver.

players.name: Alfie Jones
players.bio: Bristol-born centre-back who chose the Maple Leaf over the Three Lions. Still earning his stripes internationally with just 2 caps, Jones brings Championship-level physicality from Middlesbrough to Canada's backline.
players.facts: Jones was born in Bristol, England — making him one of several Les Rouges defenders who grew up far from Canada.

players.name: Luc de Fougerolles
players.bio: A teenager born in London who is already making waves in Belgian football with Dender. At just 20, de Fougerolles is one of the most exciting young defenders in the squad — and at his first World Cup, no less.
players.facts: Born in 2005, de Fougerolles could be playing at a World Cup before he's old enough to rent a car in most countries.

players.name: Joel Waterman
players.bio: Langley, BC's dependable defensive rock, Waterman brings experience and MLS know-how from Chicago Fire to Canada's back four. A composed reader of the game who has quietly built a solid international career.
players.facts: Waterman hails from Langley, BC — a city so Canadian it has both a Tim Hortons and a hockey rink on every corner.

players.name: Mathieu Choinière
players.bio: Quebec-born midfielder who swapped the cold of Saint-Jean-sur-Richelieu for the Californian sun of LAFC. A dynamic box-to-box presence, Choinière brings energy and flair to Canada's midfield engine room.
players.facts: Choinière and Eustáquio are LAFC teammates — Canada's midfield has a very West Coast flavour at this World Cup.

players.name: Stephen Eustáquio
players.bio: Born in Leamington, Ontario, but with Portuguese heritage, Eustáquio is Canada's midfield heartbeat. A technically gifted playmaker at LAFC, his 56 caps and big-game temperament make him indispensable to Les Rouges.
players.facts: Eustáquio chose Canada over Portugal — a decision that looks more inspired with every passing tournament.

players.name: Ismaël Koné
players.bio: Born in Abidjan, raised in Canada, and polished in Serie A with Sassuolo — Koné's journey is remarkable. At just 23, the powerhouse midfielder already has 40 caps and is a key pillar of Canada's generational squad.
players.facts: Koné was born in Ivory Coast and could have represented the Elephants — Canada's gain was West Africa's loss.

players.name: Cyle Larin
players.bio: Canada's all-time top scorer with 30 goals in 90 caps, Larin is a seasoned striker who has played across Europe and MLS. The Brampton-born forward brings World Cup experience and a clinical finishing touch to the attack.
players.facts: Larin is Canada's all-time leading scorer — the man has put the ball in the net for his country more than anyone, ever.

players.name: Jonathan David
players.bio: Brooklyn-born, Montreal-raised, and now a Juventus striker — David's journey is the stuff of legend. With 39 goals in 77 caps, Canada's No.10 is among the world's most lethal finishers and the tournament's most wanted man.
players.facts: David was born in Brooklyn, NY — technically making Canada's greatest striker an American by birth. Don't mention it.

players.name: Liam Millar
players.bio: Toronto-born winger with pace to burn and a fondness for taking on defenders. Millar has been a consistent option for Canada across 41 caps, bringing directness and creativity down the left flank for Hull City and Les Rouges.
players.facts: Millar came through Liverpool's academy — he never played for the first team, but at least he's at a World Cup now.

players.name: Tani Oluwaseyi
players.bio: Born in Abuja but proudly Canadian, Oluwaseyi has quickly made an impression at Villarreal and on the international stage. His pace and directness up front offer Les Rouges a dynamic alternative option in attack.
players.facts: Oluwaseyi shares a Villarreal connection with Tajon Buchanan — Canada have a Yellow Submarine fan club in this squad.

players.name: Derek Cornelius
players.bio: From Ajax, Ontario — yes, that Ajax — to Ibrox, Cornelius has had quite the journey. A commanding centre-back with 44 caps, he brings aerial strength and Rangers pedigree to Canada's defensive unit.
players.facts: Cornelius is from Ajax, Ontario — a town named after the Dutch club. Coincidence? His career path suggests otherwise.

players.name: Jacob Shaffelburg
players.bio: From Kentville, Nova Scotia — not exactly a football hotbed — Shaffelburg has become a genuine international threat. Six goals in 31 caps shows the LAFC winger has an eye for goal to go with his directness and energy.
players.facts: Shaffelburg hails from Kentville, Nova Scotia — proof that Canadian football talent grows in the most unexpected places.

players.name: Moïse Bombito
players.bio: Montreal-born Bombito is one of the most elegant defenders in the squad, combining composure on the ball with athletic defending. Now at Nice, the 26-year-old is a modern centre-back built for the highest level.
players.facts: Bombito's surname is so perfectly suited to a defender it almost sounds made up. It isn't. He really is that cool.

players.name: Maxime Crépeau
players.bio: Quebec's own shot-stopper and one of MLS's most reliable goalkeepers at Orlando City SC. With 32 caps, Crépeau brings experience and a safe pair of hands as Canada's experienced backup between the sticks.
players.facts: Crépeau once saved a penalty in an MLS Cup Playoff shootout — goalkeepers who do that deserve their own Wikipedia page.

players.name: Tajon Buchanan
players.bio: Electric, unpredictable, and utterly terrifying for full-backs — Buchanan is Canada's ultimate wide weapon. The Brampton-born Villarreal winger has 60 caps and 8 international goals, and his pace is simply not fair.
players.facts: Buchanan was once clocked as one of the fastest players in Serie A during his Inter Milan spell. Defenders beware.

players.name: Owen Goodman
players.bio: Romford-born shot-stopper who hasn't yet earned a senior cap but made the World Cup squad at just 22 — remarkable. Goodman is Canada's future between the posts, learning from experienced heads on the biggest stage of all.
players.facts: Goodman was born in Romford, Essex — birthplace of several England hopefuls, but he chose the Maple Leaf instead.

players.name: Alphonso Davies
players.bio: Born a refugee in Ghana, raised in Edmonton, and now Bayern Munich's flying full-back — Davies' story is extraordinary. Canada's icon brings 58 caps, 15 goals, and breathtaking pace to a World Cup on home soil. Pure magic.
players.facts: Davies was born in a Ghanaian refugee camp. He is now one of the world's best footballers. Sport doesn't get better than this.

players.name: Ali Ahmed
players.bio: Born in Bahrain but proudly Canadian, Ahmed is a versatile defender who has made his mark at Norwich City in the Championship. His 24 caps show a player building steadily into an important squad member for Les Rouges.
players.facts: Ahmed was born in Bahrain, grew up in Canada, and plays in England — his passport has earned its air miles.

players.name: Jonathan Osorio
players.bio: The heartbeat of Canadian football for over a decade, Osorio is Toronto FC's beloved captain with 90 caps and 10 goals. At 33, this World Cup on home soil is the crowning chapter of a legendary Canadian career.
players.facts: Osorio has played his entire club career at Toronto FC — one-club loyalty in MLS is rarer than a Canadian summer.

players.name: Richie Laryea
players.bio: Toronto-born and lightning-quick, Laryea is one of MLS's most dynamic right-backs with 75 caps to his name. His overlapping runs and tenacity have made him a fixture in Canada's setup for the better part of a decade.
players.facts: Laryea briefly joined Nottingham Forest and Rangers — he's done the full tour before returning home to Toronto FC.

players.name: Niko Sigur
players.bio: Burnaby's teenage dynamo who has impressed at Hajduk Split with composed, intelligent defensive midfield play. At just 22, Sigur's 19 caps suggest a player rapidly maturing into a key figure for Les Rouges.
players.facts: Sigur plays for Hajduk Split — one of the most passionately supported clubs in Croatia, a baptism of fire for any youngster.

players.name: Promise David
players.bio: Brampton's Promise David — yes, really — is delivering on his name at Union Saint-Gilloise. Three goals in just 10 caps is a ratio that turns heads, and the 24-year-old forward could be a surprise package at this tournament.
players.facts: His name is Promise David, and with 3 goals in 10 caps, he's keeping it. Canada's most aptly named player by some distance.

players.name: Nathan Saliba
players.bio: Longueuil-born Saliba is one of the most exciting young midfielders in Belgian football at Anderlecht. At 22, his two international goals in 15 caps hint at a technically gifted player ready to announce himself on the world stage.
players.facts: Saliba was born in 2004 — meaning he was still in primary school when Canada last qualified for a World Cup in 2022.

players.name: Jayden Nelson
players.bio: Toronto-born winger with electric pace and an eye for goal, Nelson has scored 3 goals in 14 caps — an impressive return for a 23-year-old still finding his feet at Austin FC. One of Les Rouges' most exciting attacking options.
players.facts: Nelson scored on his senior international debut — making an immediate impression is apparently a Toronto thing.
